detail: Authentication uses InMemoryUserDetailsManager with static user definitions in SpringSecurityConfig. This pattern is appropriate only for demos or local development; production systems need a managed user store (database, directory, or identity provider) with lifecycle controls, auditing, and credential rotation.

Usernames and plaintext password material (pwd1, pwd2) are embedded in source for user1 and user2. Even with BCrypt encoding at runtime, secrets in code leak through version control and builds; credentials should come from secure configuration, secrets management, or external identity systems.

HttpSecurity is configured with csrf().disable() globally for the application. CSRF is disabled only for /h2-console via ignoringAntMatchers, but the second chain turns off CSRF entirely. That weakens protection for any future state-changing browser flows or session-based usage beyond pure stateless Basic clients.

The security configuration extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter, which Spring Security deprecated in 5.7+ in favor of SecurityFilterChain beans and component-style configuration. Spring Boot 2.7.0 still supports it, but this blocks straightforward upgrades to newer Spring Security APIs and should be migrated when upgrading the stack.

httpBasic() is enabled, so credentials travel on every request (typically Base64 in the Authorization header). For browser-heavy or mobile apps, token-based flows (OAuth2/OIDC, JWT with proper issuance and validation) are usually preferred to reduce replay exposure and enable richer policy; Basic can be acceptable for simple internal APIs over TLS with strong policies.

Initial passwords pwd1 and pwd2 are trivially guessable and violate common password policy expectations. Even with BCrypt, weak chosen passwords remain vulnerable to offline guessing if hashes are exposed; enforce strength policy and user-driven password changes.

All secured routes use a single ROLE_USER via hasRole("USER"). There is no separation of duties (e.g., admin vs user), resource-owner checks, or scopes; authorization is coarse and may not scale if roles or data ownership rules grow.

MoodSenseController uses HttpServletRequest.getRemoteUser() for the authenticated principal when saving moods and querying distributions. This is valid when populated by the servlet container from Spring Security, but SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() is the idiomatic Spring approach and avoids subtle mismatches if authentication is accessed outside a web request context.

/h2-console/** is permitAll with CSRF ignored for that path and frameOptions sameOrigin, allowing unauthenticated access to the embedded console. If this profile ships to production or any network-exposed environment, it exposes database administration and is a serious risk; restrict by profile, IP, or remove entirely outside dev.

There are no @PreAuthorize, @PostAuthorize, @Secured, or equivalent method-security annotations in the codebase, and no @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity / @EnableMethodSecurity. All enforcement is URL-based only, so service-layer entry points or future internal callers would not get declarative authorization checks.
